hook: A 1961 volleyball-player neon that still serves above MDM's socialist-realist square
facts: Designed by Jan Mucharski in 1960, installed in 1961 on the corner building at Plac Konstytucji 5.
It advertised a sports shop (Sklep Sportowy) on the ground floor of the MDM building.
The animated neon shows a woman in a red outfit striking a ball that 'falls' through 16 sequenced tubes down the facade.
Restored and ceremonially relit on 19 May 2006 on the initiative of artist Paulina Ołowska and the Foksal Gallery Foundation.
Entered into the Masovian register of movable cultural monuments in March 2025 as evidence of PRL-era planned 'neonisation'.

sources.note: Designer, 1961 date, address Plac Konstytucji 5, coordinates 52°13'21.9"N 21°00'53.4"E, 16-sequence animation
March 2025 heritage-register listing, survival in original form

w: A volleyball player in red strikes a ball that drops through sixteen sequenced tubes down the corner of Plac Konstytucji — Jan Mucharski's 1961 neon for a sports shop, a survivor of the PRL's great age of neon. Paulina Ołowska had it relit in 2006; heritage-listed at last in 2025. Still serving, after sixty years.

hook: A working Tatar Muslim necropolis near the city centre, graves facing Mecca
facts: Founded in 1867 on the initiative of Warsaw imam Seifetdin Chosianow Sinnajew, after the older Muslim Caucasus Cemetery on Młynarska closed.
Located at ul. Tatarska 8 in Wola; it grew from about 0.5 ha to roughly 1 ha.
It serves Poland's Tatar Muslim community, descendants of Tatars who settled in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania from the late 14th century.
Notable burials include chemist Osman Achmatowicz, lawyer-politician Aleksander Achmatowicz, and Azerbaijani prince Veli bek Jedigar.
Listed as a monument in 1984 and, with five other Powązki denominational cemeteries, designated a Monument of History in 2014.

sources.note: 1867 founding, imam Sinnajew, ul. Tatarska 8, coordinates 52°15'11"N 20°57'54"E, notable burials, heritage status
Local-press feature confirming active Tatar mizar near central Warsaw

w: A working Tatar Muslim cemetery near the centre, founded in 1867, its graves turned to face Mecca — the ground of Poland's Polish-Lithuanian Tatars, settlers in the Grand Duchy six centuries back. A chemist and an Azerbaijani prince lie here. A Monument of History since 2014. Islam, quietly rooted in Wola.

hook: Poland's only active Karaim cemetery, a tiny Turkic enclave on Wola
facts: Established in 1890 with consent of the Tauride hakham, on land bought from the Orthodox parish at ul. Redutowa 34.
It is the only active Karaim cemetery in Poland, holding roughly 100 graves across about 0.11 ha.
The first burial (June 1890) was Józef Kobecki, a one-month-old infant; the oldest surviving named stone (1895) is Saduk Osipowicz Kefeli.
The Karaims arrived in Warsaw in the late 19th century, many of them Crimean tobacco-trade workers, subjects of the Russian Empire.
Buried here are leading Karaim orientalists Ananiasz Zajączkowski, Aleksander Dubiński and Włodzimierz Zajączkowski.

sources.note: 1890 founding, ul. Redutowa 34, coordinates 52°13'59"N 20°56'30"E (52.233056, 20.941667), only active Karaim cemetery, notable burials
Karaim community site corroborating history and location

w: Poland's only living Karaim cemetery — a tiny Turkic, non-Talmudic people who reached Warsaw in the late 1800s, many in the Crimean tobacco trade. Founded in 1890 on Redutowa, barely a hundred graves on a tenth of a hectare, it holds the great Karaim orientalists, the Zajączkowskis among them. A vanishing community's last small ground.

hook: The only Warsaw Ghetto street with surviving tenements on both sides
facts: Próżna is the only Ghetto street whose buildings survived on both sides — four houses at nos. 7, 9, 12 and 14.
The surviving tenements (Nożyk at 9, Neufeld at 12, Wolanowski at 14) were entered into the heritage register in 1987.
By the late 19th century the street was largely Jewish, its ground floors filled with metal-goods shops.
Its western part was inside the Warsaw Ghetto from November 1940 until excluded in March 1941.
Since 2004 the street hosts events of the Warsaw Singer Festival; from 2008-2014 large archival portraits of Polish Jews hung on the facades.

sources.note: Only two-sided surviving Ghetto street, houses 7/9/12/14, coordinates 52°14'11.6"N 21°00'19.6"E (52.236546, 21.005448), Singer Festival
Local history blog corroborating Jewish-Ghetto history and surviving tenements

w: The only street of the Warsaw Ghetto whose tenements still stand on both sides — four houses, numbers 7 to 14, where all around was levelled. Largely Jewish by the late 1800s, it fell inside the Ghetto in 1940. The Singer Festival fills it now. Próżna means empty — in name, if no longer in fact.

hook: An eclectic 1899 tenement nicknamed 'Peking' for its teeming crowds
facts: Built 1896-1899 at ul. Złota 83, corner of Żelazna, for Jewish real-estate investor Wolf Krongold.
Its Warsaw-slang nickname 'Pekin' meant an over-crowded rental tenement.
Originally three storeys, later raised to five; lower flats had marble staircases, upper ones plain wooden stairs.
Its attic once bore sculptures of a bearded old man and a muscular youth flanking the date 1899, symbolising the turn of the century.
Listed as a monument in 1989; after a 2015 fire it was restored by 2022 into 146 rental apartments.
legend: The dialect word 'Pekin' for a packed slum tenement — a far-eastern city standing in for sheer density of people — became its lasting nickname.

sources.note: 1896-1899 build, ul. Złota 83, coordinates 52°13'45"N 20°59'41"E (52.229167, 20.994722), Pekin nickname, attic sculptures, heritage no. 1398-A
Restoration coverage corroborating address and reconstruction

w: They called it Pekin — Warsaw slang for a rental block so packed it felt like a city in itself. Wolf Krongold built it on Złota in 1899, marble stairs for the lower flats, bare wood for the upper, an old man and a youth carved in the attic by the date. Burned in 2015, reborn by 2022 as 146 apartments. Density, made monument.

hook: A satire-only museum in an 18th-century orangery off Krakowskie Przedmieście
facts: Founded in 1978 by cartoonist Eryk Lipiński, who directed it until his death in 1991.
It occupies a former orangery once attached to the 18th-century Primate's (Prymas) palace, on ul. Kozia 11.
The collection holds over 20,000 caricatures and satirical drawings by Polish and foreign artists.
Lacking permanent display space, it runs temporary exhibitions drawn from its holdings.
It is the only museum in Poland devoted specifically to caricature and satirical art.

sources.note: 1978 founding by Lipiński, Kozia 11, coordinates 52°14'42"N 21°0'47"E (52.245, 21.01306), former orangery, 20,000-piece collection
Culture.pl entry corroborating dedicated caricature museum and location

w: Poland's only museum of caricature, founded in 1978 by the cartoonist Eryk Lipiński and tucked into an 18th-century orangery off Krakowskie Przedmieście. Twenty thousand satirical drawings, Polish and foreign, rotated through temporary shows for want of room to hang them all. Where Poland keeps its sense of humour. Drawn to wound.

hook: A Le Corbusier-inspired 1930s 'Glass House', AK uprising HQ and a Picasso footnote
facts: Designed by Juliusz Żórawski as a prize-winning competition entry and built 1938-1941 at ul. Mickiewicza 34/36.
The L-shaped block (wings of 125 m and 55 m) embodied Le Corbusier's five points: ribbon windows, free plan, free facade, pilotis and roof terraces.
It held 110 luxurious flats, an underground garage for 20 cars, its own boiler room and three rooftop recreation terraces — the most modern pre-war residence in Żoliborz.
From 1 August to 30 September 1944 it housed the Home Army's Żoliborz district command under Mieczysław Niedzielski 'Żywiciel'.
It was refurbished in 1948 for Pablo Picasso's visit to Warsaw and a planned banquet in his honour on the roof terrace.

sources.note: Żórawski, 1938-41, Mickiewicza 34/36, coordinates 52°16'18"N 20°59'05"E (52.271667, 20.984722), AK HQ, 1948 Picasso renovation, Le Corbusier points
Culture.pl entry corroborating architect, modernist credentials and history

w: The 'Glass House,' Juliusz Żórawski's 1938 modernist block on Mickiewicza — a textbook of Le Corbusier's five points, the most modern address in pre-war Żoliborz. In the 1944 Uprising it became the Home Army's Żoliborz command post; in 1948 it was spruced up for Picasso's visit and a rooftop banquet. Glass, with a past.

hook: The oldest building in Praga: a Loreto house-chapel that locals saved from demolition
facts: The oldest surviving church/building in Praga, at ul. Ratuszowa 5a, originating with Bernardine monks who arrived in 1617.
Its Loreto chapel (domek loretański), built 1640-1644 and likely designed by royal architect Konstantyn Tencalla, is enclosed on all four sides by a walkway.
When Napoleonic fortifications in 1811 required clearing the area, the Loreto chapel survived thanks to local resistance.
A stone by the main path is the symbolic grave of Roch Kowalski, a hero of Sienkiewicz's 'Trilogy'.
legend: Faithful to the legend that the Holy House of Nazareth was carried by angels to Loreto, the chapel reproduces that 'domek loretański' on the Praga riverbank.

sources.note: Oldest building in Praga, Bernardines 1617, Loreto chapel 1640-44, ul. Ratuszowa 5a, coordinates 52.256714, 21.0277, survival 1811, Roch Kowalski grave
Praga district authority page corroborating history and significance

w: The oldest building in Praga, where Bernardine monks settled in 1617 — and at its heart a Loreto chapel of the 1640s, a little house modelled on the one angels were said to have carried from Nazareth to Italy. When Napoleon's engineers wanted the ground cleared in 1811, locals saved it. A stone outside 'buries' a Sienkiewicz hero.
